name: Broken Function Level Authorization (BFLA)
what_it_means: The API fails to enforce permissions on actions and endpoints — allowing low-privilege users to invoke administrative, privileged, or restricted functions simply by calling the endpoint directly. Authorization is missing or implemented only at the UI layer, not the backend.
animated_example: Pressing the elevator's staff-only button and it still takes you to the restricted floor — the button is hidden on the public panel but still works if you know it exists.
quick_checks: Server-side authorization enforced on every action endpoint — no reliance on UI visibility alone
Admin routes isolated with dedicated middleware and network-level controls
HTTP method to privilege mapping defined and enforced per endpoint
MFA / step-up re-authentication required for privileged or irreversible operations
Hidden, debug, and maintenance endpoints blocked in production environments
Centralized RBAC or ABAC policy with a documented permission matrix reviewed at every release

payload_types.type: Admin Endpoint Access with Low-Privilege Token
payload_types.description: Attacker calls administrative API endpoints directly using a standard user's authentication token, exploiting missing or bypassed server-side authorization checks on those routes.
examples: POST /api/admin/users/delete with a regular user JWT — returns 200 and deletes a user account
GET /api/admin/allLogs — accessible without admin role, returning full application audit logs
PUT /api/settings/global { "maintenanceMode": true } — accepted from a non-admin token, taking the application offline
GET /api/admin/invoices/export — bulk export of all customer invoices accessible to any authenticated user
POST /api/admin/roles/assign { "userId": 99, "role": "admin" } — role assignment endpoint reachable by a standard user token
mitigation: Enforce server-side RBAC or ABAC on every admin and privileged endpoint — never rely on UI visibility, route obscurity, or client-side checks to gate access.
Prefix all admin routes under a dedicated path (e.g., /admin/, /internal/) and apply a separate authentication middleware with strict role validation to all routes under that prefix.
Maintain a centralized permission matrix mapping every HTTP method and endpoint path to required roles — review and sign off this matrix at every release cycle.
Apply network-level controls to admin APIs: restrict access by IP allowlist or require mTLS from known internal services.
Automated test: invoke every admin endpoint with a standard user token and assert 403 is returned — include this in your CI/CD security gate.

payload_types.description: Attacker changes the HTTP method on an endpoint to invoke a higher-privilege operation — e.g., switching from GET (read-only) to POST, PUT, or DELETE — exploiting authorization checks that protect some methods but not others on the same path.
examples: GET /api/reports/99 is authorized for all users — DELETE /api/reports/99 has no authorization check and deletes the record
GET /api/config authorized for read-only users — PUT /api/config overwrites configuration without a separate privilege check
OPTIONS response on /api/admin/users reveals DELETE is supported — attacker sends DELETE /api/admin/users/42 and succeeds
POST /api/users/{id}/disable works without admin check because only GET on that path was restricted
PATCH /api/users/{id} with the body { "locked": false } — available to standard users despite only GET being documented as permitted
mitigation: Apply authorization checks independently for each HTTP method on every endpoint — a GET authorization check does not implicitly protect PUT, PATCH, or DELETE on the same path.
Use an explicit HTTP method allow-list per endpoint: define which methods are permitted for each role and return 405 Method Not Allowed for any method not on the list.
Restrict OPTIONS responses to avoid advertising HTTP methods that are not fully authorization-hardened.
Test every HTTP method (GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE, OPTIONS) on every endpoint in your security test suite — include low-privilege tokens for each test.
Centralize method-level authorization in a middleware layer so new endpoints cannot be added without explicitly defining their method-permission mapping.

payload_types.type: Hidden and Debug Endpoint Direct Access
payload_types.description: Attacker discovers and directly accesses internal maintenance, debug, or development endpoints that were not removed or disabled before production deployment — often missing any authentication or authorization checks.
examples: GET /api/debug/config returns full application configuration including database credentials and API keys
POST /api/internal/rebuildIndex triggers a full database rebuild accessible without authentication from the internet
GET /api/health/details returns internal service topology, version numbers, and dependency health including internal hostnames
POST /api/test/createAdminUser — a test helper endpoint left in production that creates an admin account with no authentication
GET /actuator/env (Spring Boot) returns all environment variables including secrets when actuator endpoints are not restricted
mitigation: Remove all debug, test, and maintenance endpoints before production deployment — use feature flags or deployment profiles to exclude these routes from production builds.
Block internal and actuator endpoints at the network layer (firewall, security group) so they are unreachable from the public internet even if accidentally deployed.
Scan deployed production APIs regularly using automated discovery tools to detect endpoints present in production that are not in the approved API inventory.
Never expose framework-level diagnostic endpoints (Spring Actuator, Django Debug Toolbar, Flask Debug) in production — disable them in production configuration profiles.
Apply authentication and authorization to any operational endpoint that must remain in production (health checks, metrics) — restrict to monitoring system IPs only.

payload_types.type: Vertical Privilege Escalation via Role Parameter
payload_types.description: Attacker manipulates role, permission, or privilege-level parameters in API requests to elevate their own access level or gain capabilities intended only for higher-privilege users.
examples: POST /api/auth/token { "username": "user", "password": "pass", "role": "admin" } — token issued with admin role if the role claim is accepted from the request body
GET /api/resources?accessLevel=admin — endpoint returns admin-level resources when the access level is supplied as a query parameter without server-side validation
POST /api/invitations { "email": "x@y.com", "role": "superadmin" } — invitation issued with elevated role if role is not validated against the inviter's own permissions
PUT /api/users/me { "permissions": ["read","write","delete","admin"] } — permissions array accepted and stored without checking against the caller's own permission scope
JWT refresh endpoint accepts a role_override claim in the refresh token body and issues an access token with that elevated role
mitigation: Never derive a user's role or permissions from client-supplied request parameters — always resolve them from the authenticated session, user record, and RBAC policy on the server.
Validate role assignment requests against the assigning user's own role: a user cannot grant roles or permissions exceeding their own — enforce this at the business logic layer.
Reject any request that includes role, permissions, or privilege-level fields in contexts where the caller does not have explicit authorization to set those fields.
Log all role change operations with before/after values, actor identity, and target user — alert on attempts to self-assign elevated roles.
Audit all token issuance endpoints to confirm roles and permissions are resolved from authoritative server-side sources, not from any client-supplied claim.

payload_types.description: Attacker invokes API endpoints intended for a different user role — such as a buyer calling a seller-only endpoint, a free-tier user calling premium-tier endpoints, or a read-only user calling write endpoints — exploiting insufficient role boundary enforcement.
examples: Buyer account calls POST /api/seller/listings to create a product listing — seller-only endpoint accepts a buyer token
Free-tier user calls POST /api/premium/export — premium feature available to any authenticated user regardless of subscription tier
Read-only service account calls POST /api/data/bulk-delete — write operations not restricted by service account scope
Customer support role calls POST /api/billing/refund/{id} — financial operation only intended for billing admins but accessible to support role
Unapproved vendor calls GET /api/partner/pricing — partner-tier endpoint returning discounted pricing accessible to regular API consumers
mitigation: Model user roles at sufficient granularity to distinguish between all meaningful permission boundaries in your application — buyer vs. seller, free vs. premium, read vs. write.
Apply role checks at the business logic layer for every function, not just route-level middleware — a route middleware may check authentication but business logic must check authorization.
Enforce subscription and feature tier checks server-side: never rely on client-side feature flags or UI hiding to gate premium or restricted features.
Conduct regular privilege audit reviews: for each endpoint, document which roles may access it and include automated tests asserting that all other roles are rejected.
Use deny-by-default authorization: if an endpoint does not have an explicit allow rule for a role, that role is denied — never use an allow-by-default posture.

how_to_test: Step 1: Map all API endpoints including admin, internal, and debug paths — then invoke each with a standard user token and assert 403 is returned
Step 2: Test every HTTP method (GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE) on each endpoint with a low-privilege token — assert methods not permitted for that role return 403 or 405
Step 3: Attempt to access /admin/, /internal/, /debug/, /actuator/ paths from the internet — assert all are blocked or return 403/404
Step 4: Submit role, permissions, or privilege-level fields in request bodies and assert they are rejected or silently stripped
Step 5: Test cross-role access: as a buyer call seller endpoints, as a free-tier user call premium endpoints — assert 403 in all cases
Step 6: Test step-up authentication bypass: call privileged operations (bulk delete, role assignment, config change) without completing MFA challenge — assert 403
Step 7: Verify audit logging: perform unauthorized function access attempts and confirm each is logged with actor identity, endpoint, and method
expected_outcomes: Standard user tokens return 403 for all admin, internal, and debug endpoints
HTTP methods not permitted for a role return 403 or 405 — never execute the operation
Role and permissions fields in request bodies are rejected or ignored — never persisted
Cross-role feature access is blocked at the business logic layer, not just the UI
All BFLA attempt events are logged with actor identity, target endpoint, and HTTP method
minimal_example: Normal user → POST /api/admin/rebuildIndex → 403 Forbidden; admin with valid MFA → 200 OK; standard user with role:admin in body → 403 (role field ignored)
mitigation: Enforce server-side RBAC or ABAC on every function endpoint — never rely on UI visibility, hidden routes, or client-side checks alone to gate access to privileged operations
Maintain a centralized permission matrix mapping each HTTP method and endpoint to required roles; review and sign off this matrix at every release; apply deny-by-default authorization posture
Require step-up MFA re-challenge for privileged or irreversible operations regardless of active session state; log all unauthorized function access attempts with full actor and endpoint context
Isolate admin APIs onto separate routes protected by network-level controls (IP allowlist, mTLS); remove all debug and test endpoints before production deployment; scan for shadow endpoints regularly
